prompt: Reaching for TypeScript is a reflex; plain JavaScript feels under-dressed to you.
Most of your day is spent on screens people actually look at.
Starting a new screen, you open a component framework like React first.
You could happily spend a whole day getting one screen’s structure and spacing exactly right.
You build components in isolation, with a workshop like Storybook always open.
You think in reusable systems — one component should serve a hundred screens.
A component without documented props and design tokens does not feel finished to you.
You will open a screen reader to check a feature, not just the browser.
Profiling a real page with real data is a normal part of your week.
The performance problems that nag you happen while the page is running in the browser.
You can explain why an avoidable re-render can cost more than the request that triggered it.
A two-minute CI build feels like a bug with your name on it.
The API and the database behind your screens are usually yours to change too.
Your client code is built to land in an app store.
App-store review cycles and OS updates shape how you plan a release.
You’d happily write a feature twice to use each platform’s native language.
Push notifications, biometrics, deep links — a platform’s own APIs are your bread and butter.
Shipping one codebase to both app stores is the trade-off you happily make.
You wire up a typed client between frontend and backend before writing much else.
Shipping a whole feature end to end is what makes the work satisfying for you.
Owning a feature from the form on screen to the row in the database is your idea of a good week.
You sweat the shape of a payload the way others sweat a layout.
Infrastructure as code is simply how your team runs everything.
Automating away repetitive toil is a goal you actively chase.
Your job is keeping the platform itself running and scaling.
The systems you run are large enough that a manual change is something you actively forbid.
Building and running the platform is your home turf; observability and on-call are the next layer up.
Giving teams a paved road to ship on is a goal you actively design for.
You’re happiest deep in provider-level infrastructure and IaC.
Availability zones and traffic patterns weigh equally with the tooling you reach for.
When you pick up an infrastructure task, are you writing IaC or designing network topology?
You can explain why a Terraform workspace structure matters for scaling.
A zone going dark does not concern you; your architecture absorbs it.
Whether you are managing the cluster itself or what developers build on it, your work is fundamentally about platforms.
Does your focus land on keeping the cluster running or on what developers build on top of it?
You know your cluster's resource limits the way a DBA knows their indices.
Developers deploy to production from the CLI without naming a single Kubernetes concept.
Error budgets and SLOs are tools you actually use, not slides you have seen.
Instrumentation and dashboards are where your day goes, not the pager.
Real-time dashboards and deep historical forensics are both part of how you think about systems.
Are metrics and traces your natural habitat, or do logs and events tell the stories you care about?
Five minutes and a query are enough to find where a service is losing time.
Your logs tell the incident story more truthfully than any dashboard ever could.
Preventing incidents and learning from them when they happen are both central to your role.
Would you rather design systems that fail gracefully or lead the team through crisis when they do not?
Before scaling a service, you have already written its retry and timeout strategies.
A retro you ran left the team aligned on what actually failed and what to change.
Other engineers are the people who actually use what you ship.
Keeping attackers out is part of your daily work.
A finding caught in the IDE or a pull request beats one caught in production every time.
When you look for risk, you read the application’s own code first.
Your security mindset blends prevention and discovery, defense and offense, as partners.
When you find a vulnerability, did you stop it in the code or uncover it in the live system?
You threat-model a feature sketch before you open an IDE.
You find the edge case that breaks the threat model.
From artifact signing to key rotation, your security posture is built end to end.
Does hardening the build pipeline claim more of your focus than locking down access and secrets?
Every binary in production can be traced back to its source commit and build logs.
Your team rotates every secret without a ticket or a Slack conversation.
Open source is your natural way to get the tools you build into people’s hands.
You measure your impact in the daily friction you remove for other engineers.
Whether you save time in the developer's loop or automate it away in the pipeline, friction reduction is your north star.
Does your work optimise how developers work or how the build pipeline runs at scale?
Your tool turns a five-step manual dance into a single, memorable command.
A developer merges; everything else happens without a human decision.
The entire chain from language runtime to developer command line is where your expertise flows.
Do you spend more time in compilers and runtimes or shipping CLIs that developers rely on?
You understand the performance cost of the code your compiler generates.
Your build tool's help text is the only guide anyone needs.
Python is where you live for most of your work.
A result you can’t reproduce isn’t finished, as far as you’re concerned.
Your day is closer to models and AI than to data pipelines and analytics.
Your work runs on experiments, and you expect plenty of them to fail.
You’d rather train and run your own models than build on a hosted one.
A framework like PyTorch or TensorFlow is installed and in use on your machine.
You love digging into a model’s internals — its layers, attention, and gradients.
Reading academic papers is how you keep sharp in your domain.
Exploring novel architectures matters more to you than proving they work at scale.
You measure your impact by papers written or techniques contributed, not by models in production.
A new architecture paper can pull you in as fast as a new benchmark score.
Model cards, deployment logs, and monitoring dashboards are equally important to you.
Evaluating whether a model is production-ready fascinates you more than operating the serving layer.
You've built an evaluation framework that catches regressions the way a test suite catches bugs.
A model is only interesting to you once it is serving traffic without falling over.
Shipping an AI feature, you wire up context and tools and leave the model weights alone.
The product layer — prompts, tools, and agent behaviour — is where you spend your time.
Watching an LLM solve multi-step problems through tool use still feels like magic.
Designing tool chains and agent flows excites you more than perfecting prompt wording.
Designing an agent that sequences tools correctly without explicit prompts feels like genuine progress.
Fine-tuning a prompt until a model consistently outputs your exact format feels like mastery.
Embedding model trade-offs are conversations you have as often as most engineers discuss latency.
Optimizing how documents flow into the index interests you more than what lives in it.
You spend more effort on document chunking and refresh strategies than on tuning LLM prompts.
Improving retrieval accuracy through smarter indexing and ranking is where you find satisfaction.
Your work is consumed by other systems as often as by people.
You take pride in building the pipelines that move data.
You carry a pager for your pipelines, just like a backend engineer does for services.
Events stream into your systems and you process them the moment they land.
Real-time data freshness is baked into your system design from day one.
Reliably capturing changes from source systems fascinates you more than processing those events.
A connector that catches every source change without data loss or double-counting is your achievement.
A correctly windowed join across a distributed stream that handles late events gracefully is elegant.
Your schema design philosophy shapes how you approach every transformation and pipeline.
Open table formats and schema-on-read appeal to you more than managed warehouse appliances.
You chose Iceberg for schema evolution and time-travel because they align with your governance philosophy.
A normalized dimensional model with clear lineage and documentation is an artifact you're proud of.
SQL is your home base; Python is the tool you grab when you need it.
You’d reach for a BI tool and a clean data model before a notebook of statistics.
You consider data freshness and query performance as part of your craft.
Building a governed metric layer matters more to you than crafting individual dashboards.
When everyone stops arguing about which metric to use because there's one source of truth, you've won.
Watching someone understand a complex data story through your dashboard is genuinely fulfilling.
Hypothesis testing and experimental design are woven into how you think.
Building models that predict the future excites you more than understanding what happened.
A model with strong cross-validation and lower error than the baseline is a win worth celebrating.
Proving with statistical rigor that a treatment worked is more satisfying than any model prediction.
Your work targets a device, a game, or a team — rarely a typical web service.
You still write more code than you review, delegate or pitch.
Your daily toolchain is built around native SDKs, game engines or embedded runtimes.
What you build runs on a screen and is meant to be played.
You have shipped at least one game to a public store or platform.
You judge your work by one question: is it fun to play?
Playtesting a build with real players teaches you more than any design doc.
You benchmark a shader the way other engineers benchmark an API.
Memory footprint and interrupt timing are numbers you watch as closely as throughput.
You enjoy wringing every last cycle out of a single chip.
Bare metal is where you are comfortable; an operating system is a luxury you design around.
Your code reads sensors and drives motors as often as it talks to a server.
You answer to people — a team or a market — first, and to a block of code second.
You’re growing a team inside a company someone else started.
Your calendar is mostly one-on-ones and team rituals, with little design or architecture time.
Your instinct is to invest in people before you invest in the architecture.
Your team shipping well without you in the room is the clearest sign of your best work.
Setting the technical direction the team can run with is squarely your responsibility.
Fundraising, customer calls and runway math are recurring items on your calendar.
Building the company and its product is what you’re really chasing.
Debugging production at 2am and pitching investors at 9am can happen on the same day for you.
Turning hard technology into something developers love to use is the work you gravitate to.
